one_line_takeaway: LLM API routers are an unmonitored, unauthenticated man-in-the-middle over agent tool-call traffic, and the authors find real routers already exploiting this in the wild (1/28 paid, 8/400 free routers injecting malicious code; 401 of 440 poisoned-relay sessions already running with tool auto-approval) while showing that today's best client-side defense, a fail-closed policy gate, can be fully bypassed by a defense-aware attacker.
key_finding: Malicious routers already operate in both paid and free commodity markets: 1 of 28 paid routers and 8 of 400 free routers inject malicious code into returned tool calls; 2 free routers deploy adaptive evasion (one waits for 50 prior calls before activating, another restricts injection to autonomous YOLO-mode sessions targeting Rust/Go projects); 17 free routers trigger follow-on abuse of researcher-owned AWS canary credentials; 1 free router drains ETH from a researcher-owned private key. The poisoning studies show ostensibly benign routers are transitively exposed: the single intentionally leaked OpenAI key generated 100M GPT-5.4 tokens across >7 Codex sessions (with at least one session exposing multiple distinct credentials); the 20-domain/20-IP weak-relay decoys absorbed 40k+ unauthorized access attempts from 147 IPs (6 JA3 fingerprints), served roughly 2B GPT-5.4/5.3-codex tokens (~13 GB visible traffic), and exposed 99 credentials across 440 Codex sessions spanning 398 projects/hosts -- all 440 sessions were command-injectable and 401 were already running in auto-approve (YOLO) mode, meaning basic payload injection (no adaptive evasion needed) would have sufficed. On the Mine artifact, none of the 4 tested agent frameworks implement response-integrity verification; AC-1 achieves 100% rewrite compatibility, AC-1.a 99.6%, AC-2 extractor coverage 100%, with median proxy overhead of 0.013 ms/request against 820 ms median upstream latency. Of the three defenses: the fail-closed policy gate blocks 100% of AC-1/AC-1.a samples at a 1.0% false-positive rate and 2.3 microsecond decision cost, but is fully bypassed (100%) by an adaptive attacker using allowlisted domains or pre-positioned local stagers; response-side anomaly screening (IsolationForest) flags 89.0% of AC-1, 50.0% of AC-1.a, and 50.9% of AC-1.b samples at 6.7% false positives, dropping sharply at stricter FP budgets (43.5% AC-1 detection at 1% FP); transparency logging does not prevent manipulation but costs only ~1.26 KB/entry (12.0 MB per 1,000 sessions).
core_contribution: The paper presents the first systematic study of the LLM API router as an adversarial supply-chain intermediary. Because agent clients route tool-calling requests through third-party routers (e.g., LiteLLM, OpenRouter, and commodity resold-access services) that terminate client-side TLS and re-originate a separate connection upstream, the router has full plaintext application-layer access to prompts, tool definitions, API keys, and tool-call responses with no cryptographic integrity binding what the model produced to what the client executes. The authors (1) formalize this trust boundary and define a taxonomy of Adversarial Router Behaviors -- two core attack classes, AC-1 (response-side payload injection) and AC-2 (passive secret exfiltration), plus two adaptive-evasion variants, AC-1.a (dependency-targeted injection, e.g. typosquatting a package name inside an install command) and AC-1.b (conditional/triggered delivery based on session features); (2) run an empirical ecosystem measurement across 28 paid routers (bought from Taobao, Xianyu, and Shopify storefronts) and 400 free routers (built on the sub2api and new-api templates), plus two poisoning studies using an intentionally leaked researcher-owned OpenAI key and 20 weak-password relay decoys; and (3) build 'Mine,' a research proxy implementing all four attack classes against four public agent frameworks (OpenClaw, OpenCode, Codex, Claude Code), which they use to evaluate three deployable client-side defenses (a fail-closed policy gate, response-side anomaly screening via IsolationForest, and append-only transparency logging).
threat_model: Attacker operates a malicious LLM API router, or has compromised a legitimate one via supply-chain compromise, insider access, or server-side exploitation (the paper cites the March 2026 LiteLLM dependency-confusion incident as a real-world precedent). Because the client voluntarily configures the router as its API endpoint, the router is an intentional application-layer man-in-the-middle: it terminates client TLS and originates a separate upstream TLS session, so no TLS downgrade or cert forgery is needed. The router can read, retain, rewrite, or fabricate request/response bodies, headers, tool definitions, prompts, API keys, and returned tool-call payloads across OpenAI-, Anthropic-, and Gemini-style interfaces, and may keep cross-request state to selectively activate. The paper explicitly assumes standard TLS between router and upstream provider and no compromise of model weights or inference logic, and explicitly excludes prompt injection, model backdoors, client-side malware, denial of service, and pure model substitution as out of scope (these are noted as compositional but distinct). A formal weakest-link chain-integrity property is given: if any router in a multi-hop chain is dishonest, no integrity guarantee holds for the composed chain, and downstream honest routers cannot detect or undo an upstream router's tampering.
limitations: The authors state these themselves: (1) the corpus targets the most active publicly reachable commodity router markets (mostly Chinese-language marketplaces/communities); enterprise and invite-only deployments are explicitly out of scope and left as future work. (2) They could not always recover exact template versions or default configurations for the free-router corpus, so observed abuse is not attributed to a specific sub2api/new-api release. (3) Detection is finite black-box probing -- a router may implement additional latent conditional-delivery triggers the probes never activated, and conditional delivery (AC-1.b) is explicitly noted as fundamentally hard for any finite audit to rule out. (4) In the leaked-key poisoning study, the authors cannot always distinguish, from provider-side logs alone, whether observed downstream reuse came from direct router-operator reuse, unrelated third-party reuse, or reuse deep inside a longer relay chain. (5) Unauthorized access attempts against the weak-router decoys are a mix of opportunistic scanning and targeted relay reuse; the authors explicitly do not treat every access event as evidence of a targeted supply-chain attack. (6) All three defense evaluations (Section 7) are run on synthetic artifact-generated benign/attack corpora, not field/production traffic -- the authors explicitly flag this as controlled artifact-side evaluation rather than field data. (7) The policy gate's 100% detection is undermined by a 100% adaptive bypass rate on a simple benchmark once the attacker uses allowlisted domains or local stagers, which the authors call the main negative finding of that section. (8) Cross-framework compatibility results measure only that a tampered payload reaches the client in valid format, not end-to-end execution rates, since downstream permission prompts or sandboxes may still block the resulting action.

datasets_used: 28 paid OpenAI/Anthropic-compatible routers purchased from Taobao, Xianyu, and Shopify-hosted storefronts
400 free routers collected from public communities, built predominantly on the sub2api (11k GitHub stars) and new-api (25.4k stars, 1.25M Docker pulls) templates
TerminalBench 2.0 tasks plus custom tasks used to generate realistic agent tool-call traffic for router probing
1 researcher-owned OpenAI API key intentionally leaked on Chinese forums, WeChat, and Telegram groups
20 weak-password/vulnerable claude-relay-service, Sub2API, and CLIProxyAPI decoy deployments across 20 domains and 20 IPs
4 public agent frameworks tested with the Mine artifact: OpenClaw, OpenCode, OpenAI Codex, Anthropic Claude Code

Synthetic defense-evaluation corpora: 1,000 benign + 500 AC-1 + 500 AC-1.a shell commands (policy gate); 2,000 synthetic benign tool calls for IsolationForest training plus 3,352 benign / 301 AC-1 / 294 AC-1.a / 53 AC-1.b held-out samples (anomaly screening); 1,000 synthetic OpenAI-style sessions of 10 tool calls each (transparency log storage benchmark)
quantitative_results: 1 of 28 paid routers and 8 of 400 free routers inject malicious code into returned tool calls
2 free routers deploy adaptive evasion triggers (50-call warm-up; YOLO-mode + Rust/Go project targeting)
17 of 400 free routers touch researcher-owned AWS canary credentials; 1 drains a researcher-owned ETH wallet
Leaked OpenAI key: 100M GPT-5.4 tokens served, >7 Codex sessions exposed

Weak-relay decoys (20 domains + 20 IPs): 40k+ unauthorized access attempts from 147 IPs / 6 JA3 fingerprints; ~2B GPT-5.4/5.3-codex tokens served; ~13 GB visible downstream traffic; 99 credentials exposed across 440 Codex sessions spanning 398 projects/hosts; all 440 sessions command-injectable; 401 already in YOLO (auto-approve) mode

Mine artifact vs. 4 agent frameworks (1,000 requests/module/framework): 0/4 frameworks implement response-integrity checks; AC-1 rewrite compatibility 100%; AC-1.a rewrite compatibility 99.6%; AC-2 extractor coverage 100%; buffered streaming compatible on 4/4; median proxy overhead 0.013 ms/request vs. 820 ms median upstream latency

Policy gate defense: blocks 100% of AC-1 and AC-1.a synthetic samples at 1.0% false-positive rate, 2.3 microsecond median decision cost; 100% bypassed by an adaptive attacker using allowlisted domains / local stagers in a simple benchmark

Anomaly screening (IsolationForest) at 6.7% FP: 89.0% AC-1 detection, 50.0% AC-1.a, 50.9% AC-1.b; at 1% FP budget: 43.5% AC-1, ~0% AC-1.a, 9.4% AC-1.b; at 5% FP: 86.7% AC-1, 28.6% AC-1.a, 49.1% AC-1.b; ablation shows removing the shell-risk feature drops AC-1 detection to 17.6% and AC-1.a to 4.4%
Transparency log storage cost: 12.0 MB per 1,000 sessions, ~1.26 KB per entry
baselines_compared: No prior systematic baseline exists for this attack surface; the paper positions itself as the first systematic study of malicious LLM API routers

The three deployable defenses (policy gate, anomaly screening, transparency logging) are compared against each other on coverage of AC-1/AC-1.a/AC-1.b/AC-2, not against an external prior-work defense baseline
The March 2026 LiteLLM dependency-confusion supply-chain incident is used as a real-world existence proof/motivating precedent for the AC-1/AC-2 primitives, not as an experimental baseline
